title: Recommended workflow for SELinux cross-compat between macOS and Linux (Fedora)
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

Opening in response to guidance from #13631

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I am working on a solution to replace an old docker-compose file using podman. Some engineers use Fedora, some macOS and we would like a solution that can support both rootless containers on the former and podman machine on the latter, however there seems to be a fundamental incompatibility in that SELinux tagging is required in the containerized workflow whereas it is not supported in the virtualized one. My question is how to support these two conflicting usecases, (or even if it is supported w/o switching to virtiofsd?).

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

```yml
# Save the output of this file and use kubectl create -f to import
# it into Kubernetes.
#
# Created with podman-3.4.4
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  labels:
    app: services
  name: services
spec:
  containers:
    - name: services-database
      image: docker.io/library/mysql:8
      ports:
        - containerPort: 3306
          hostPort: 3306
        - containerPort: 33060
          hostPort: 33060
      env:
        - name: MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD
          value: root
    - image: docker.io/localstack/localstack:latest
      name: services-localstack
      ports:
        - containerPort: 4566
          hostPort: 4566
      volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /docker-entrypoint-initaws.d:z
          name: localstack-init
        - mountPath: /img:z
          name: mdisrupt-image
  volumes:
    - name: localstack-init
      hostPath:
        path: ./scripts/aws
        type: Directory
    - name: mdisrupt-image
      hostPath:
        path: ./src/test/resources/img
        type: Directory
```

1. get a machine running fedora, and one running macOS
2. attempt to run the above service file `podman play kube service.yml`
3. the setup won't run on macOS
4. remove the `:z` labelling to support macOS
5. the setup won't run on fedora

**Describe the results you received:**

As above

**Describe the results you expected:**

Ideally I would expect to find a single file that can support cross platform volume mounts 

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```

Version:      4.0.3
API Version:  4.0.3
Go Version:   go1.18
Built:        Fri Apr  1 10:28:59 2022
OS/Arch:      darwin/arm64

OS/Arch:      linux/arm64
```

```
Version:      3.4.4

Go Version:   go1.16.8
Built:        Wed Dec  8 15:45:07 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

Thanks! :)

comments.body: Edit: my apologies while I was struggling with this yesterday, having spun up a fresh test env today it seems both machines are able to support the same file sans labels. I will close shortly if I'm not able to recreate.
